title: Efficacy of Smartphone-Based Mindfulness Applications for Anxiety Reduction in Adolescents
research_question: Do unguided smartphone-based mindfulness and meditation applications reduce anxiety symptoms compared to standard care or inactive digital controls in adolescents?

pico.population: adolescents (ages 12 to 18 years) exhibiting mild to moderate anxiety symptoms, generalized anxiety, or elevated stress levels
pico.intervention: unguided, smartphone-based digital health interventions focusing on mindfulness, meditation, or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) principles
pico.comparator: waitlist control, treatment as usual (TAU), or inactive digital controls (e.g., educational apps, passive tracking apps)
pico.outcome: reduction in anxiety symptoms measured by validated psychological scales (e.g., GAD-7, SCAS), user adherence rates, and self-reported well-being
pico.study_design: randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and quasi-experimental study designs

inclusion_criteria.text: Study must focus on adolescent populations strictly between the ages of 12 and 18.
Intervention must be delivered primarily via a mobile application (smartphone or tablet) without mandatory real-time human therapist interaction.
Study must report quantitative changes in anxiety or stress using validated psychological measurement tools.
Study must include a distinct control or comparison group (waitlist, standard care, or placebo app).

exclusion_criteria.text: Studies evaluating in-person therapy, telehealth video sessions, or web-based interventions requiring a desktop computer.
Populations with severe, clinically diagnosed psychiatric disorders requiring inpatient treatment or pharmacological intervention.
Studies involving concurrent pharmacological interventions where the effect of the app cannot be isolated.
